inert: adjective. Unable to move or act; inanimate.

inti: noun. The currency of Peru between 1985 and 1991, replacing the sol.
into: preposition. To or towards the inside of.
ion: noun. An atom or group of atoms bearing an electrical charge, such as the sodium and chlorine atoms in a salt solution.

net: noun. A mesh of string, cord or rope.
nit: noun. The egg of a louse.

nitro: noun. The univalent NO 2 functional group.

norite: noun. A granular crystalline rock consisting essentially of a triclinic feldspar (such as labradorite) and hypersthene.
not: adverb. Negates the meaning of the modified verb.
one: noun. NATO & ICAO radiotelephony clear code (spelling-alphabet name) for the digit 1.
ore: noun. Rock or other material that contains valuable or utilitarian materials; primarily a rock containing metals or gems for which it is
orient: noun. The part of the horizon where the sun first appears in the morning; the east.
ort: noun. A fragment; a scrap of leftover food; any remainder; a piece of refuse.
rein: noun. A strap or rope attached to a bridle or bit, used to control a horse, other animal or young child.
rent: noun. A payment made by a tenant at intervals in order to lease a property.

retro: adjective. Of, or relating to, the past, past times, or the way things were.
riot: noun. A tumultuous disturbance of the public peace by a large group of people, often involving violence or damage to property.
rioter: noun. One who riots; part of the unruly violent crowd causing a riot.
rot: verb. To suffer decomposition due to biological action, especially by fungi or bacteria.
rote: noun. Mechanical routine; a fixed, habitual, repetitive, or mechanical course of procedure.
roti: noun. A kind of unleavened flatbread commonly consumed in South Asia and the Caribbean.

tin: noun. A malleable, ductile, metallic element, resistant to corrosion, with atomic number 50 and symbol Sn.
tine: noun. A spike or point on an implement or tool, especially a prong of a fork or a tooth of a comb.
tinier: adjective. comparative form of tiny: more tiny
tire: verb. To become sleepy or weary.
toe: noun. Each of the five digits on the end of the human foot.
ton: noun. Any of various units of mass, originally notionally equal to the contents of a tun, particularly: The short ton of 2000 pounds (about
toner: noun. Powder used in laser printers and photocopiers to form the text and images on the printed paper.
tonier: adjective. comparative form of tony: more tony
tor: noun. A craggy outcrop of rock on the summit of a hill, created by the erosion and weathering of rock.
torii: noun. A traditional Japanese gate at Shinto shrines, symbolically marking the transition from the profane to the sacred.
trier: noun. One who tries; one who makes experiments or examines anything by a test or standard.
trio: noun. A group of three people or things.

eir: determiner. Belonging to em, gender-neutral third-person singular possessive adjective, equivalent to the singular their and coordinate
ert: verb. To incite; urge on; encourage.
iter: noun. A passage, especially the passage between the third and fourth ventricles in the brain; the cerebral aqueduct.
neti: noun. Nasal irrigation performed using a neti pot as part of Hatha yoga.
oie: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Okolie.
oni: noun. A Japanese evil spirit or demon.
retin: verb. To coat with tin again.
rio: noun. Alternative form of ryō (“Japanese ounce”).

trie: noun. An ordered tree data structure that is used to store an associative array where the keys are usually strings.
